musicalLanguage.description: "Tiny Dancer" (1971) is one of Elton John's most beloved piano ballads. The harness's key-detection pass confirms its stated C major decisively, with a statedKeyFit of 0.888. The confidence-gated windows spell a clean, entirely diatonic C major vocabulary: the tonic C major itself (10% of windows considered), F major as IV (6%), G major as V (6%), A minor seventh as vi7 (3%), and Bdim as vii° (2%) — a textbook harmonic palette with no borrowed or chromatic surprises. The harness's literal repeat detection finds no identical or near-identical measures anywhere in the piece's 114 measures, so this annotation leans on the section lens for structure instead. The arrangement bookends itself in silence: both hands rest completely at measure 1 and again at the final measure, 114. The left hand's own lowest note in the piece, E1, arrives much later, at measure 77.
musicalLanguage.structure: A silent opening measure (measure 1) gives way to the main material, with the section lens marking nine distinct structural regions across the piece, the largest spanning measures 42-66 (25 measures, novelty 2.238). The C-F-G-Am7 progression governs the harmony throughout, with the busiest measures (94, 59, 49, 51, 29, all 36-40 onsets) distributed across several of those sections rather than concentrated in one. The piece closes, as it opened, in silence at measure 114.
keyMoments: The stated C major fits this piece's content at 0.888, a very strong correlation, and the confidence-gated windows confirm a clean, entirely diatonic vocabulary — tonic, IV, V, vi7, and vii° with no chromatic surprises.
Both hands rest completely at measure 1 and again at measure 114, the piece's very first and very last bars — the arrangement bookends its 114 measures in matching silence.

No identical or near-identical measures exist anywhere in this piece — a reminder that 'no literal repeats' describes what the strictest repeat test can find, not a judgment that the piece lacks structure; the section lens finds nine distinct regions instead.

The busiest measures (94, 59, 49, 51, 29, each 36-40 onsets) are spread across several different structural sections rather than clustering in one — this piece distributes its technical demand instead of saving it for a single climax.
teachingGoals: Learn the C-F-G-Am7 progression (I-IV-V-vi7) that anchors this piece — confirmed at a very strong 0.888 fit, it is safe, clean diatonic ground for building basic pop-piano harmony.

Practice using the section lens's nine structural regions to organize learning this piece, since no literal repeated measures exist to lean on — treat each region, the largest spanning measures 42-66, as its own practice unit.
Build even technical control across multiple busy passages (measures 29, 49, 51, 59, 94) rather than expecting one obvious hardest section — this piece spreads its demands out.
Practice the silent opening and closing measures (1 and 114) with real intentionality — a matching silence at both ends is a deliberate structural choice, not empty space to rush past.
styleTips: Listen for the clean C-F-G-Am7 progression resolving simply and directly — this piece doesn't need chromatic tension to be effective, and adding some would work against its character.
Play the opening and closing silent measures (1 and 114) as a matched pair — let the ending genuinely echo the stillness of the beginning.

Feel each of the section lens's nine structural regions as its own small arc — with no literal repeats to lean on, treating each region as a distinct musical idea makes the piece easier to hold in memory.

Try pacing your dynamic energy across all five busy measures (29, 49, 51, 59, 94) evenly rather than saving your loudest playing for just one of them — the piece itself distributes its demands this way.
